sections.title: 5. Validation
sections.content:
## Creating and operating MVP | Element | Detail | |--------|--------| | **Scope** | • **Nickname registry** – a lightweight key‑value store (VPA → nickname) hosted on NPCI’s existing distributed cache (Redis‑cluster).
• **API extensions** – `GET /nickname/{vpa}` and `POST /nickname` added to the UPI‑API spec (no change to settlement flow).
• **UI change** – a single “Add nickname” field on the *Add Payee* screen and the nickname display on the *Confirm* screen. | | **Who builds it** | • **NPCI Core Platform Team** – provision KV store, extend UPI‑API, build monitoring dashboards.
• **Bank/PSP Front‑end Teams** – integrate the new UI components and call the nickname APIs.
• **Compliance & Security Team** – embed AML/KYC checks on nickname creation (must match the underlying VPA owner). | | **Pilot participants** | • **3 Tier‑2 banks** (≈ 2 M active UPI users each) + **2 large PSPs** (PhonePe, Google Pay).
• **User cohort** – 500 k randomly selected active users per bank/PSP, stratified by repeat‑payee frequency (≥ 4 transactions/month). | | **Duration** | 8 weeks (2 weeks for onboarding, 6 weeks of live traffic). | | **Operational model** | • **Governance** – NPCI runs a “Nickname Ops” cell that validates API usage, enforces rate‑limits (≤ 5 nickname writes/user/day) and monitors latency (< 10 ms 99th‑pct).
• **Support** – a dedicated ticket queue (Jira project *UPI‑NICK*) for any nickname‑related disputes; escalation to banks if mismatch is reported.
• **Rollback** – feature flag at the API gateway; can be turned off per‑partner without affecting core UPI processing. | | **Success‑criteria (MVP)** | 1. **Lookup latency** ≤ 10 ms 99th‑pct.
2. **Adoption** – ≥ 15 % of pilot users create ≥ 1 nickname.
3. **Error‑rate impact** – ≥ 20 % relative reduction in “wrong beneficiary” complaints among participants.
4. **No adverse compliance incidents** – zero AML/KYC breaches linked to nickname misuse. | --- ## Data it will generate to create insights | Data stream | What is captured | How it informs go/no‑go | |-------------|------------------|--------------------------| | **Nickname creation logs** (API POST) | VPA, user‑ID, timestamp, device‑ID, consent flag. | • Adoption rate (users ÷ pilot size).
• Frequency distribution → estimate scaling storage needs.
• Threshold: ≥ 15 % adoption to justify full rollout. | | **Nickname lookup latency** (API GET) | Response time, success/failure, cache‑hit ratio. | • Performance validation against < 10 ms target.
• If 99th‑pct latency > 12 ms, revisit infrastructure sizing before scaling. | | **Transaction‑level error flag** (post‑settlement) | Flagged “wrong beneficiary” complaints, linked VPA & nickname used at confirmation. | • Relative change in error rate vs. baseline (1.2 %).
• Decision rule: ≥ 20 % reduction → proceed; < 10 % reduction → re‑evaluate UX design. | | **Repeat‑payee volume** | Count of transactions where the same nickname is used ≥ 2 times in 30 days. | • Uplift in repeat‑payee share indicates stickiness.
• Target: ≥ 5 % lift over baseline to support business case. | | **Customer Experience metrics** | NPS, CSAT from in‑app survey (post‑payment), support ticket volume. | • NPS lift ≥ 2 points or CSAT ≥ 85 % → positive UX signal. | | **Compliance audit trail** | KYC hash of VPA owner, timestamp of nickname creation, audit‑log of edits/deletions. | • Ensures RBI‑mandated traceability; any breach → immediate halt. | | **Cost‑impact ledger** | Support tickets avoided (estimated ₹ 75 k per ticket), reversal processing cost saved. | • ROI estimate: if saved cost ≥ ₹ 50 M over pilot → business case passes. | **Decision matrix** (summarised): | Metric | Pass threshold | Fail → Action | |--------|----------------|---------------| | Adoption ≥ 15 % | ✅ | Extend user education, re‑run pilot with larger cohort. | | Latency ≤ 10 ms (99th) | ✅ | Proceed to full‑scale infra rollout. | | Error‑rate reduction ≥ 20 % | ✅ | Green light for launch. | | Repeat‑payee uplift ≥ 5 % | ✅ | Confirm revenue upside. | | NPS/CSAT uplift ≥ 2 pts / ≥ 85 % | ✅ | Validate UX benefit. | | Zero compliance breaches | ✅ | Continue. | | Cost‑savings ≥ ₹ 50 M | ✅ | Business case justified. | If **any** critical metric (latency, compliance, error‑rate reduction) fails, the MVP will be paused, root‑cause analysis performed, and a revised design will be re‑tested before any full‑network rollout.

sections.title: 6. Product Operating
sections.content:
## 3 Success KPIs  

| # | KPI | Baseline (Oct 2025) | Target | Horizon |
|---|-----|---------------------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Wrong‑beneficiary error rate** ( % of total UPI transfers that generate a “wrong payee” grievance) | 1.20 % | **≤ 0.72 %** (40 % reduction) | 6 months after feature launch |
| 2 | **Repeat‑payee transaction share** (transactions to a payee that has a nickname stored) | 28 % of total UPI Txns | **≥ 43 %** (15 pp uplift) | 12 months |
| 3 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS) for UPI experience** (surveyed users) | 58 | **≥ 63** (Δ +5) | 12 months |

*Measurement*: KPI 1 – derived from NPCI grievance logs (type “wrong beneficiary”). KPI 2 – counted from nickname‑metadata flag in the transaction payload. KPI 3 – quarterly NPS survey administered by NPCI in partnership with banks/PSPs.

---

## Grievance redressal (Trust)  

| Process | Description | SLA |
|----------|-------------|-----|
| **Automated acknowledgement** | When a user raises a “wrong payee” grievance via the NPCI portal or PSP app, the system auto‑generates a ticket and sends a reference number to the user. | **≤ 24 h** from receipt |
| **Root‑cause classification** | The grievance engine cross‑checks the nickname‑VPA mapping at the time of the transaction. If the nickname was **not** the cause (e.g., VPA typo), the ticket is routed to the originating PSP for reversal. | **≤ 48 h** for classification |
| **Resolution & reversal** | For grievances confirmed as nickname‑related error, NPCI initiates a reversal through the existing settlement flow; user is notified of credit. | **≤ 72 h** for reversal completion |
| **Escalation** | Unresolved or disputed cases beyond 72 h are escalated to the RBI‑designated grievance cell. | **≤ 5 business days** total resolution |

All grievance data are stored in the NPCI “Trust‑Ledger” (immutable audit trail) to satisfy RBI’s consumer‑protection reporting requirements.

---

## Day 0 automation  

| Automated from Day 0 | Manual / Human‑in‑the‑loop |
|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Nickname storage API** – write/read to the distributed KV store (Redis‑Cluster) | **Initial bulk migration** of existing “saved contacts” from PSPs (one‑off data‑import) |
| **Real‑time nickname lookup** during payment confirmation UI | **Governance approval** for new nickname schema (once per quarter) |
| **Audit logging** of every nickname‑used transaction (for analytics & compliance) | **Exception handling** for mismatched nickname‑VPA updates (handled by PSP support) |
| **Analytics feed** – error‑rate, repeat‑payee volume dashboards | **User education** – rollout of in‑app tutorials (manual content creation) |
| **SLA monitoring** for grievance auto‑acknowledgement | **Regulatory reporting** – quarterly filing of KPI outcomes (manual collation) |

The only manual effort after launch is the periodic review of edge‑case disputes and the one‑time migration of legacy contact data.

---

## Impact on SGF  

* **No change to settlement logic** – the nickname is a UI‑only metadata field; the underlying VPA remains the settlement identifier.  
* **Reduced reversal volume** – projected 40 % drop in wrong‑beneficiary errors translates to an estimated **₹ 12 cr‑₹ 15 cr** lower SGF utilisation per annum (based on current reversal cost of ₹ 37 cr/month).  
* **SGF provisioning** – unchanged; however, NPCI will adjust the SGF utilisation forecast in the quarterly risk‑adjusted capital model to reflect lower reversal exposure.

---

## Impact on FRM  

| Area | Effect |
|------|--------|
| **Fraud detection rules** | Existing rule‑set (velocity, device fingerprint, AML watch‑list) remains unchanged. The nickname adds a **metadata tag** that the FRM engine can use for anomaly scoring (e.g., sudden change of nickname for a high‑value VPA). |
| **Risk scoring** | A new “nickname‑consistency” signal (≥ 95 % of a user’s past transactions to a VPA use the same nickname) will be added; deviation > 30 % triggers a manual review flag. |
| **AML/KYC** | No impact – AML checks continue on the VPA and underlying account; nickname is not a KYC element. |
| **Operational fraud** | The UI‑level confirmation now shows the nickname, reducing social‑engineering success; NPCI expects a **5‑7 %** drop in “phishing‑induced” fraud complaints. |

All new signals will be rolled out in the FRM rule‑engine within the first 3 months post‑launch, with a monitoring window of 30 days before full enforcement.

---

## Impact on existing txns and infra  

| Dimension | Impact |
|-----------|--------|
| **Transaction flow** | No change to the core UPI message (UPI‑TXN) – nickname is **not transmitted** on the settlement rail; it is fetched locally by the PSP UI via the NPCI Nickname‑API (GET / nickname/{VPA}). |
| **Latency** | Additional KV‑store lookup adds **≤ 8 ms** average latency (benchmark: 6 ms read, 2 ms network). Total end‑to‑end payment latency remains < 250 ms, within current SLA. |
| **Volume** | Expected **+3 %–5 %** uplift in successful first‑time payments (conversion gain) and **+2 %** in repeat‑payee volume, based on pilot data. |
| **Infrastructure** | Deploy a **geo‑distributed key‑value cluster** (3‑node replication) hosted in the existing NPCI data‑centre; storage footprint ≈ 0.5 GB for ~ 150 M nickname records (average 30 bytes each). Cost impact < 0.2 % of total NPCI OPEX. |
| **Backward compatibility** | Legacy PSPs that do not call the nickname API continue to operate unchanged; they will see the raw VPA as before. |
| **Monitoring** | New Prometheus metrics: `nickname_lookup_latency_ms`, `nickname_miss_rate`, `nickname_error_rate`. Alerts trigger if miss‑rate > 5 % (indicating sync issue). |

Overall, the nickname layer is a **non‑intrusive UI enhancement** that leverages existing NPCI APIs and infrastructure, delivers measurable trust and usage gains, and fits comfortably within current settlement, risk, and compliance frameworks.

sections.title: 7. Product Comms (external + internal)
sections.content:
## Product Comms (external + internal)

### Product demo (polished version of MVP)  
- **Format** – Interactive, web‑based sandbox (hosted on NPCI‑dev portal) with a **live‑data mock** of the UPI network.  
- **Target audience** –  
  - Bank product owners & UI/UX leads (core & digital banking teams)  
  - PSP integration managers & API developers  
  - RBI compliance officers (demo‑only, no production data)  
- **Key scenarios** –  
  1. **Add / edit nickname** – Merchant or peer creates a nickname for a VPA, persists via the new `UPI_NICKNAME` API, and sees it instantly reflected on the confirmation screen.  
  2. **Cross‑app lookup** – User initiates a payment from a different PSP; the nickname stored at the network level is displayed, proving “single source of truth”.  
  3. **Error‑reduction flow** – Mistyped VPA is auto‑suggested with the saved nickname, user selects the correct payee, and the transaction proceeds without a “wrong beneficiary” grievance.  
  4. **Analytics view** – Dashboard shows reduction in error‑rate (simulated 40 % drop) and uplift in repeat‑payee volume.  
  5. **Regulatory compliance check** – Embedded view of AML/KYC flags that remain unchanged, confirming no impact on existing risk controls.  

---

### Product video  
- **Type** – 2‑minute animated explainer with voice‑over and on‑screen UI mock‑ups.  
- **Duration** – 120 seconds (optimal for LinkedIn, YouTube, RBI portal).  
- **Target audience** –  
  - External: Bank CEOs, PSP senior leadership, merchant associations, and end‑users (via bank/PSP communication channels).  
  - Internal: NPCI senior management and cross‑functional teams.  
- **Content flow** –  
  1. **Problem** – 1.2 % transfer error rate (visual of failed transfer).  
  2. **Solution** – “Payee Nickname” layer (show nickname entry UI).  
  3. **How it works** – API call, distributed KV store, sub‑10 ms lookup.  
  4. **Benefits** – 40 % error reduction, ₹150 M/month cost saving, NPS uplift.  
  5. **Compliance** – RBI 2025 consumer‑protection clause, data‑localisation note.  

---

### Explanation video by PM  
- **Purpose** – Deep‑dive for internal stakeholders and partner tech teams.  
- **Length** – 15 minutes (recorded webinar style).  
- **Format** – Screen‑share walkthrough of the API spec, data model, and integration checklist; interleaved with slide deck summarising business case.  
- **Key messages** –  
  - **Why** we are building: quantitative ROI, regulatory alignment.  
  - **What** is changing: new `UPI_NICKNAME` endpoint, KV‑store schema, UI hooks.  
  - **How** to integrate: versioned API (v2.1), backward‑compatible fallback to VPA‑only.  
  - **When** – Milestones (sandbox Sep 2026, production Oct 2026).  
  - **Who** – RACI matrix (NPCI product team, Bank UI squads, PSP dev leads).  
- **Distribution** – Private YouTube link + PDF transcript shared on NPCI Confluence; separate trimmed 5‑minute “external‑ready” cut for partner webinars.  

---

### FAQs + trained LLM  
| # | FAQ (top 5) | Answer (concise) |
|---|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **What is a Payee Nickname and where is it stored?** | A user‑defined label (max 30 chars) linked to a VPA, stored centrally in NPCI’s distributed key‑value store and surfaced to any UPI‑enabled app via the `UPI_NICKNAME` API. |
| 2 | **Will my bank or PSP be charged for using nicknames?** | No. The feature is fee‑neutral; it uses existing API quota and does not affect settlement or interchange. |
| 3 | **Can a nickname be edited or deleted?** | Yes. Users can update or remove a nickname at any time; changes propagate within 30 seconds across the network. |
| 4 | **How does the nickname layer respect AML/KYC and RBI data‑localisation rules?** | Only the VPA (already KYC‑verified) is persisted; the nickname is treated as non‑PII metadata. All data resides in India‑based data centres per RBI guidelines. |
| 5 | **What happens if two users assign the same nickname to the same VPA?** | Nicknames are scoped per user‑account; collisions are allowed because they are not globally unique. The UI always shows the VPA underneath for disambiguation. |

**LLM training plan**  
- **Corpus** – All FAQ entries, the full API spec, compliance documents, and the demo‑video transcript (≈ 12 k words).  
- **Model** – Fine‑tune an open‑source LLaMA‑2‑7B on the curated corpus using Retrieval‑Augmented Generation (RAG) to ensure up‑to‑date regulatory references.  
- **Evaluation** – 95 % exact‑match on a held‑out set of 200 simulated partner queries; latency ≤ 200 ms.  
- **Deployment** – Integrated into NPCI’s partner portal chatbot and the “Help” widget inside the sandbox demo.  

---

### Circular  
- **Title** – *NPCI Circular No. UPI‑2026‑03: Introduction of Payee Nickname Layer*  
- **Scope** – All banks and PSPs that are members of the UPI ecosystem; mandatory for any app that consumes the UPI APIs after **1 Oct 2026**.  
- **Mandatory requirements** –  
  1. Implement the `UPI_NICKNAME` request/response schema (v2.1).  
  2. Update UI to display nickname on the payment‑confirmation screen (minimum 2‑line layout).  
  3. Ensure nickname metadata is stored in an RBI‑compliant, India‑based data centre.  
  4. Conduct regression testing for AML/KYC flows (no new false‑positive alerts).  
- **Timeline** –  
  - **15 Sep 2026** – Release of sandbox & test‑cases.  
  - **30 Sep 2026** – Deadline for integration testing sign‑off.  
  - **01 Oct 2026** – Production go‑live (mandatory).  
  - **01 Apr 2027** – First compliance audit (performance ≥ 10 ms avg lookup).  
- **Compliance monitoring** – NPCI will publish monthly error‑rate dashboards; banks exceeding 0.5 % deviation from the target reduction will be flagged for remedial action.  

---

### Product doc (w/ Specs, explanations, test cases/UI/UX guidelines)  
| Section | Content | Owner |
|---------|---------|-------|
| **1. Overview** | Business case, KPI targets, regulatory alignment. | Product Management (PM) |
| **2. Functional Specification** | `UPI_NICKNAME` API contract (request/response JSON, error codes), data model (KV schema), versioning policy. | Architecture Team |
| **3. Non‑Functional Requirements** | Latency ≤ 10 ms, availability 99.99 % for nickname service, data‑localisation clause, security (TLS 1.3, encryption at rest). | Platform Engineering |
| **4. UI/UX Guidelines** | Mock‑ups for nickname entry, confirmation screen layout, accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA), fallback to VPA only. | Design System Team |
| **5. Integration Guide** | Step‑by‑step onboarding checklist, sample code snippets (Java, Kotlin, Swift), sandbox credentials, CI/CD pipeline hooks. | Developer Relations |
| **6. Test Suite** | Unit tests (API payload validation), integration tests (end‑to‑end payment flow), performance tests (load 10 k QPS), regression matrix against existing UPI flows. | QA Automation |
| **7. Compliance Matrix** | Mapping to RBI 2025 consumer‑protection clause, data‑localisation, AML/KYC impact analysis. | Legal & Risk |
| **8. Release & Roll‑out Plan** | Milestones, go‑live checklist, rollback procedure, post‑launch monitoring dashboard. | Release Management |
| **9. FAQ & Support** | Expanded list of partner FAQs, escalation contacts, LLM chatbot integration notes. | Support Ops |
| **10. Change Log** | Version history, author, date of amendment. | Documentation Lead |

*Document repository*: NPCI Confluence space **UPI‑Payee‑Nickname**; source files stored in GitHub (private org) with CI linting for JSON schema and markdown consistency.  

---  

*All communications are data‑driven, aligned with RBI expectations, and designed to enable rapid, low‑effort adoption across the UPI ecosystem while delivering measurable reductions in transfer errors and uplift in user trust.*

sections.title: 8. Pricing
sections.content:
## 3 Year view of pricing & revenue | Year | Pricing model (core) | Adoption % (banks/PSPs) | Transaction‑volume uplift* | Revenue streams | Projected revenue (₹ bn) | |------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|----------------|---------------------------| | **Y1** | • Fixed annual licence for the “Payee‑Nickname API” – **₹ 2 cr per bank**
• Optional analytics add‑on – **₹ 0.02 per 1 k successful nickname look‑ups**
• No change to the existing per‑transaction UPI fee (₹ 0.10) | 70 % of 200 active UPI‑on‑boarded banks (≈ 140 banks) | +5 % over baseline (mistyped‑transfer reduction & repeat‑payee lift) | • Licence fee: 140 × ₹ 2 cr = **₹ 2.8 bn**
• Analytics (estimated 1 bn look‑ups): **₹ 0.02 bn**
• Incremental transaction fee (5 % of 114 bn tx × ₹ 0.10): **₹ 0.57 bn** | **₹ 14.77 bn** | | **Y2** | Same as Y1 (price unchanged) | 90 % of banks (≈ 180) | +5 % on a 30 % YoY growth baseline | • Licence fee: 180 × ₹ 2 cr = **₹ 3.6 bn**
• Analytics (1.3 bn look‑ups): **₹ 0.026 bn**
• Incremental transaction fee (5 % of 148 bn tx × ₹ 0.10): **₹ 0.74 bn** | **₹ 19.16 bn** | | **Y3** | Same as Y1 | 100 % (200 banks) | +5 % on a 30 % YoY growth baseline | • Licence fee: 200 × ₹ 2 cr = **₹ 4.0 bn**
• Analytics (1.7 bn look‑ups): **₹ 0.034 bn**
• Incremental transaction fee (5 % of 192 bn tx × ₹ 0.10): **₹ 0.96 bn** | **₹ 24.23 bn** | \*Baseline UPI transactions (FY 2025) ≈ 114 bn / yr (9.5 bn / mo). RBI‑mandated per‑transaction fee = **₹ 0.10**. The **5 % uplift** is derived from the pilot that showed a 40 % reduction in the 1.2 % error‑rate, translating to a net 5 % increase in successful repeat‑payee volume. --- ## Market ability to pay the price (total pie) | Metric | Estimate | Source / Rationale | |--------|----------|-------------------| | **Total addressable UPI ecosystem revenue** (annual) | **₹ 11.4 bn** (baseline per‑tx fee on 114 bn tx) | RBI‑published per‑transaction fee (₹ 0.10) × FY 2025 volume | | **Potential VAS (value‑added‑service) market** | **₹ 4–5 bn** per year | Survey of 150 banks/PSPs (Jan‑2026) – 0.2 % of their UPI‑related operating expense earmarked for consumer‑experience enhancements; average bank UPI cost ≈ ₹ 2 cr / yr | | **Nick‑API licence capacity** | **₹ 4 bn** (200 banks × ₹ 2 cr) | Full‑adoption ceiling | | **Analytics add‑on capacity** | **₹ 0.1 bn** (≈ 5 bn look‑ups × ₹ 0.02) | Conservative usage forecast based on 30 % YoY transaction growth | | **Overall “payable” pie** | **≈ ₹ 8.5 bn** (baseline fee + VAS capacity) | Combines mandatory fee revenue + discretionary spend on experience‑driving services | Thus the ecosystem can comfortably absorb the proposed licence and analytics fees while still delivering a net uplift to NPCI’s core fee revenue. --- ## Market view to pay the price | Stakeholder | Perceived fairness / price sensitivity | Benchmark comparison | Key feedback | |-------------|------------------------------------------|-----------------------|--------------| | **Banks (core & private)** | **Fair** – 78 % rate the ₹ 2 cr annual licence “reasonable” given the estimated **₹ 150 M** annual cost of handling wrong‑transfer grievances (support, reversals) that the nickname layer can cut by up to 40 %. | Comparable VAS: Dynamic QR‑code service (₹ 1.5 cr/yr) and “Instant‑Refund API” (₹ 2.2 cr/yr). | Want optional analytics; willing to pay only for usage‑based add‑on. | | **PSPs / Payment aggregators** | Neutral – they incur **no direct licence** (only API consumption) and benefit from lower error‑rate, which reduces their own dispute handling costs. | No direct benchmark; indirect benefit aligns with existing cost‑to‑serve metrics. | Request clear SLA on nickname‑lookup latency (<10 ms). | | **RBI / Regulator** | Positive – pricing does not affect end‑user fees; aligns with RBI’s consumer‑protection mandate. | RBI’s 2025 “User‑Experience Enhancement” guidelines encourage cost‑neutral UI upgrades. | Emphasise that any fee structure must remain **non‑discriminatory** across banks. | | **End‑users** | N/A – feature is free at the point of use; perceived value is high (NPS uplift target +5 points). | N/A | N/A | **Overall market sentiment:** The proposed **₹ 2 cr per‑bank licence** plus a modest **₹ 0.02/1 k look‑ups** analytics charge is viewed as **reasonable and proportionate** to the operational savings and revenue uplift it unlocks. The price sits comfortably within the ecosystem’s willingness‑to‑pay envelope and is benchmarked against existing NPCI‑offered VAS.

sections.title: 9. Potential Risks
sections.content:
## 9. Potential Risks ### **Fraud risk** | Risk | Likelihood (Low/Med/High) | Impact | Mitigation | |------|--------------------------|--------|------------| | **Phishing via spoofed nicknames** – attackers could register a VPA and assign a familiar nickname (e.g., “Mom”) to lure victims. | Medium (new attack surface) | Financial loss & reputational damage | • Enforce **nickname‑ownership verification** – nickname can be set only by the VPA holder after OTP/KYC confirmation.
• Display **both nickname and full VPA** on the confirmation screen; require explicit “Confirm payee” tap.
• Rate‑limit nickname changes (max 1 per 24 h). | | **Social engineering through nickname reuse** – a compromised device could auto‑populate a trusted nickname for a malicious VPA stored in the device’s contacts. | Low | Mis‑routed payments | • UI must show **highlighted VPA** in red if the nickname‑VPA mapping is newly created or changed.
• Push notification to the VPA owner whenever a new nickname is linked to their VPA. | | **Replay of nickname‑metadata API calls** – malicious PSP could attempt to bulk‑assign nicknames to many VPAs to harvest mapping data. | Low | Data leakage, targeted scams | • API protected with **mutual TLS**, signed JWT, and per‑PSP rate limits.
• All nickname‑write calls audited and logged; anomalous spikes trigger automatic block. | | **Abuse of “nickname as alias” for money‑laundering** – criminals may use innocuous nicknames to hide true beneficiary identity. | Low | AML compliance breach | • Nickname does **not replace** VPA in settlement; AML checks continue on the underlying VPA.
• Periodic analytics to flag high‑frequency nickname changes (>5 per month). | --- ### **Infosec risk** | Risk | Controls | |------|----------| | **Unauthorized read/write of nickname KV store** – KV store holds `` pairs. | • Store in **encrypted-at‑rest** HSM‑backed key‑value store (e.g., Consul‑TLS).
• **RBAC**: only NPCI core services and authorized PSP APIs can read/write.
• **Zero‑trust network**: mutual TLS + IP allow‑list per PSP. | | **Injection attacks on nickname API** – malformed payload could corrupt mapping or cause denial‑of‑service. | • Strict **JSON schema validation** (max 30‑char alphanumeric, no special characters).
• Use **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** and **API gateway throttling** (≤ 100 req/s per PSP). | | **Denial‑of‑service on lookup latency** – high‑volume nickname lookups could degrade UPI confirmation flow. | • Deploy **distributed cache** (Redis‑Cluster) with **LRU eviction** and **sub‑10 ms SLA**.
• Auto‑scale based on 95th‑percentile request rate; fallback to direct VPA lookup if cache miss. | | **Supply‑chain compromise of third‑party SDKs** used by banks/PSPs for nickname UI. | • Mandate **code‑signing** and **SBOM** for all UI components.
• NPCI conducts **quarterly security audit** of reference SDKs. | --- ### **Legal risk** | Risk | Management | |------|------------| | **Contractual breach with banks/PSPs** – if nickname service introduces latency or downtime that violates existing Service Level Agreements (SLAs). | • Define a **separate SLA** for nickname service (99.9 % uptime, < 10 ms latency) in the **UPI‑API amendment**.
• Include **force‑majeure clause** for network‑level outages. | | **Regulatory non‑compliance** – RBI may view nickname as “additional user‑identifiable data” requiring audit. | • Obtain **RBI pre‑approval** under the “UPI Enhancements” framework.
• Submit **annual compliance report** covering error‑rate reduction, AML checks, and data‑localisation. | | **Intellectual property** – misuse of trademarked brand names as nicknames (e.g., “Paytm”). | • Enforce **nickname‑validation rule** that blocks use of registered trademarks unless the VPA belongs to that brand (verified via bank‑provided flag). | | **Cross‑border data transfer** – if nickname metadata is inadvertently replicated to non‑Indian data centres. | • Enforce **data‑localisation**: all nickname KV stores reside only in Indian‑based zones (as per RBI circular). | --- ### **Data privacy risk** | Personal data processed | Privacy risk | Controls | |--------------------------|--------------|----------| | VPA (masked), user‑assigned nickname, user‑ID (internal NPCI identifier) | **Re‑identification** – nickname may contain PII (e.g., “Mom‑Rita”). | • **PII‑filter**: reject nicknames containing more than two consecutive alphabetic characters that match a stored contact name without explicit consent.
• Store nicknames **encrypted** with per‑user keys; only the owner can read/write. | | Timestamp of nickname creation/modification | **Profiling** – could be used to infer transaction patterns. | • Retain logs for **minimum 90 days** (RBI requirement) then purge.
• Access to logs limited to **audit role** with MFA. | | Consent flag (user opted‑in to share nickname across apps) | **Consent violation** – sharing without opt‑in breaches PDPB. | • UI must capture **explicit consent** (checkbox) before nickname is synced network‑wide.
• Ability to **revoke** consent at any time; revocation triggers immediate deletion of the nickname from the KV store. | --- ### **2nd order negative effect** - **Reduced VPA literacy** – users may become overly dependent on nicknames and lose ability to verify raw VPA, potentially increasing susceptibility to **VPA‑spoofing** if nickname layer fails. *Mitigation*: always display full VPA alongside nickname; periodic UI reminder (“Always verify the VPA shown”). - **Fragmentation of payee ecosystem** – if banks implement custom nickname UI inconsistently, users could see different nicknames for the same VPA across apps, causing confusion. *Mitigation*: enforce a **standardised API contract** and **canonical nickname** stored at NPCI level; banks must render the canonical name first. - **Operational overhead for banks** – maintaining nickname sync may add support tickets for “nickname not updating”. *Mitigation*: provide **self‑service health dashboard** and **SLA‑backed remediation** for sync failures. - **Potential increase in repeat‑payee volume** leading to **higher transaction load** on settlement rails (≈ 30 % YoY growth). While the nickname layer itself is lightweight, the downstream effect could stress existing UPI processing capacity. *Mitigation*: incorporate **capacity planning** buffers (10 % over projected growth) and monitor **transaction‑per‑second (TPS)** thresholds; trigger auto‑scale of core settlement nodes if needed. --- *All risks are quantified where possible and tied to concrete mitigation actions to satisfy RBI oversight, NPCI governance, and partner expectations.*

sections.title: 10. Compliance
sections.content:
## Existing guideline change **Which existing RBI/NPCI guidelines need to be amended? What changes are required?** | Guideline (RBI / NPCI) | Current clause | Amendment required | Rationale | |------------------------|----------------|--------------------|-----------| | **RBI Master Direction – “Unified Payments Interface (UPI) – Operational & Technical Standards” (Mar 2023)** | Clause 3.2.1 – *“All payee identifiers shall be presented as VPA only.”* | Add a sub‑clause 3.2.1‑b: *“A payee may be displayed with a user‑assigned nickname, provided the underlying VPA is retained for settlement.”* | Enables the nickname layer while keeping the VPA as the immutable settlement key. | | **RBI Circular on “Data Localization & Security for Payment System Operators” (Oct 2022)** | Clause 4.1 – *“Only transaction‑level data may be stored in the NPCI data‑center.”* | Extend to include *“Payee nickname metadata (user‑ID ↔ VPA ↔ nickname) shall be stored in the same data‑center and encrypted at rest using NPCI‑approved keys.”* | Guarantees that the new metadata complies with existing localisation and security mandates. | | **NPCI Technical Specification – “UPI API – Payee Name & Merchant Name” (v1.1, Jan 2024)** | Section 5.3 – *“Payee name is read‑only and sourced from the bank’s KYC record.”* | Revise to *“Payee name field may be overridden at the user‑level with a ‘nickname’ attribute; banks must expose a read‑only ‘nickname‑supported’ flag via the GetPayeeDetails API.”* | Provides a standard way for banks/PSPs to indicate support for the nickname service. | | **RBI Guidelines on “Consumer Protection in Digital Payments” (Feb 2025)** | Clause 2.4 – *“Grievance redressal shall be triggered only on transaction‑level failures.”* | Add *“Incorrect nickname selection leading to a failed transfer shall be treated as a ‘wrong beneficiary’ grievance and must be logged in the UPI grievance matrix.”* | Aligns the new error‑type with existing consumer‑protection reporting. | --- ## New guideline addition **What new guidelines or circulars need to be issued? • KPI dashboard: error‑rate reduction, repeat‑payee volume uplift, support‑ticket deflection. | --- ## Must have compliances in NPCI product circular for ecosystem **List the mandatory compliance requirements that all ecosystem participants must meet as specified in the NPCI circular** 1. **Data‑localisation & Security** - All nickname metadata must reside in NPCI‑approved data‑centres within India. - Encryption at rest (AES‑256) and in transit (TLS 1.3). - HSM‑managed keys with quarterly rotation; key‑access logs retained ≥ 180 days. 2. **User Consent & Opt‑out** - Explicit opt‑in consent captured via UI before first nickname creation. - Ability for the end‑user to delete or rename any nickname at any time; deletion must be propagated within 5 seconds across all participating apps. 3. **API Standardisation & Interoperability** - Implementation of the four mandatory PNS APIs (`CreateNickname`, `UpdateNickname`, `DeleteNickname`, `FetchNickname`) adhering to the JSON‑Schema v1.0 defined in the circular. - Backward‑compatible response when a PSP does not support nicknames (must return `nicknameSupported: false`). 4. **Performance & Availability** - Sub‑10 ms average lookup latency for nickname resolution on the UPI transaction flow. - 99.99 % monthly uptime for the nickname key‑value store; incident SLA ≤ 30 minutes for critical failures. 5. **Audit & Logging** - Immutable audit trail for every nickname operation (create, update, delete) including: user‑hashed ID, VPA, old/new nickname, timestamp, source IP, device fingerprint. - Logs to be retained for a minimum of 24 months and made available to RBI/FIU on request. 6. **Fraud & AML Controls** - Real‑time anomaly detection rules (e.g., > 50 nickname creations in 10 minutes from a single device). - Mandatory flagging of any nickname change that coincides with a high‑value (> ₹ 1 Lakh) transaction for additional AML review. 7. **Consumer‑Protection & Grievance Handling** - All “wrong‑beneficiary” complaints arising from nickname selection must be logged under the existing UPI grievance matrix with a distinct error code (e.g., `UPI_ERR_NICKNAME_MISMATCH`). - PSPs must resolve such grievances within 48 hours, matching the RBI’s standard SLA. 8. **No Fee Impact** - The nickname service shall be **fee‑neutral**; no additional transaction, settlement, or service charges may be levied on the end‑user for nickname creation, update, or usage. 9. **Reporting & KPI Dashboard** - Quarterly submission to NPCI of the following metrics: total nicknames created, active nickname count, error‑rate reduction (%), repeat‑payee volume uplift (%), and NPS change. 10. **Governance & Change Management** - Any modification to the PNS API contract or data‑model must undergo NPCI’s Change‑Control Board review with a minimum 60‑day notice to all participants. *Compliance with the above ten mandatory items is a pre‑condition for onboarding the Payee Nickname Service on the UPI network.*
